title_en: Ethics, Manners, and the Character of the Prophet
content_en:
The final major sections of Sharh al-Sunnah address the ethical and spiritual dimensions of Islamic life — the hadiths about character, manners, relationships, and the spiritual struggles of the believer. These sections reflect al-Baghawi's understanding that the Sunnah is not merely a legal code but a comprehensive guide to virtuous character and spiritually meaningful life.

The Character Hadiths

Al-Baghawi collects the hadiths about Islamic character traits that the Prophet encouraged and embodied. These include truthfulness and its indispensability as the foundation of reliable social life; generosity and the spiritual dangers of miserliness; patience (sabr) as the master virtue that makes all other virtues possible in the face of adversity; gratitude (shukr) for divine blessings; and justice in all relationships, commercial and personal. He presents the Prophet's own character as the standard, citing hadiths in which the Prophet's wife Aisha described his character as the Quran made manifest — his character and his moral guidance were one.

The Treatment of Others

A substantial section collects the hadiths about how Muslims should treat others: parents, spouses, children, neighbors, slaves (in the historical context), non-Muslim subjects, animals, and the natural world. Al-Baghawi's treatment of the animal welfare hadiths is particularly notable: the Prophet's prohibition on causing unnecessary suffering to animals, his commendation of those who treat animals mercifully, and his warning that a woman was sent to Hell for imprisoning a cat and a man was granted Paradise for giving water to a thirsty dog — all these reflect an Islamic ethic that extends moral consideration beyond human beings.

The Dangers of the Tongue and Heart

Al-Baghawi collects the hadiths about the spiritual dangers of speech — lying, backbiting, slander, and idle talk — and the diseases of the heart — pride, envy, hatred, and the love of worldly things. The famous hadith in which the Prophet grabbed his tongue and said 'Hold this still for me' and the companion asked 'Are we really accountable for what we say?' and the Prophet replied 'Does anything throw people into Hellfire on their faces except the harvests of their tongues?' — this tradition and others like it frame the ethical struggle as primarily a struggle over the use of speech and the purification of inner states.

Al-Baghawi's Enduring Contribution

Sharh al-Sunnah was widely read in the Khurasani and later the broader Sunni tradition as a reliable, comprehensive source for hadiths on all areas of Islamic life. Its combination of sound hadith transmission with practical legal and spiritual commentary made it useful for scholars, students, and educated laypeople alike. Al-Baghawi's other major work, Maalim al-Tanzil (his Quran commentary), and his fiqh compendium Al-Tahdhib fi Fiqh al-Imam al-Shafi'i, together with Sharh al-Sunnah, establish him as one of the most comprehensively productive scholars of the fifth and early sixth centuries of the Islamic era, whose works continued to shape the Sunni scholarly tradition in the East for generations after his death.
